title: Handling Reasoning Failures
concept: Designing fallbacks when the planning model gets stuck in logical loops or contradictions.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: what happens when an AI agent searches deeper for the 'best' plan?

options.text: It gets slower but often more accurate, so you find a sweet spot
It always gets faster and more accurate at once
Depth has no effect on speed

exercises.explain: More search depth usually means better answers but longer waits. The goal is the sweet spot where extra accuracy is still worth the extra time.

cards.title: Stuck in a Loop
cards.body: Sometimes a planning AI keeps going in circles: 'Do A, then B, then A, then B...' It never finishes. Real agents need a way to escape.

cards.title: What Is a Fallback?
cards.body: A fallback is a backup plan that kicks in when the main reasoning fails. It catches the agent when it loops, contradicts itself, or gets stuck.

cards.title: Like a Safety Rope
cards.body: A climber wears a rope so a slip doesn't end in disaster. A fallback is the AI's safety rope: when its logic slips, it catches instead of crashing.

cards.title: A Loop Limit
cards.body: An agent plans a trip but keeps re-checking the same two flights forever. A fallback says: 'After 5 tries, stop and pick the best option so far.'

cards.title: Myth: Smart AI Never Gets Stuck
cards.body: Even strong models can loop or contradict themselves. Good design assumes failures will happen and plans an exit ahead of time.

cards.title: Loop vs Fallback
cards.body: Without an exit, the agent spins forever. With a fallback, it notices trouble and switches to a safe path.

left.text: Agent repeats A→B→A→B and never answers.

right.text: After a limit, agent stops and gives its best safe answer.

exercises.prompt: Why do agents need fallbacks for reasoning failures?

options.text: So they can escape loops or contradictions instead of getting stuck forever
To make the AI run slower on purpose
To delete all of the agent's memory
To stop the agent from ever using tools

exercises.explain: Fallbacks give the agent an escape hatch. When logic loops or contradicts itself, the fallback lets it recover and still produce a usable answer.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Setting a maximum number of reasoning steps is one way to break out of an infinite loop.
exercises.explain: A step or loop limit forces the agent to stop after a set number of tries, so it can't spin forever. Then it uses the best option it has found.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about backup plans.
exercises.sentence: A fallback is a backup plan that activates when the main reasoning fails.
exercises.explain: A fallback is exactly that backup plan. It only activates when the agent's normal reasoning breaks down, keeping the system reliable.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of handling a stuck agent was AI-generated?

options.text: When a planning agent detects it is repeating the same steps, a fallback rule can halt the loop and return the best partial plan found so far.
just let it keep going lol it will figure it out eventually
turn it off and on, thats what i always do

exercises.explain: The AI answer is clear, structured, and technically correct: detect the loop, halt it, return the best partial result. The others are casual guesses without a real strategy.

exercises.prompt: An agent is planning a schedule but keeps flip-flopping: it books 2pm, then says 2pm is wrong and books 3pm, then says 3pm is wrong and books 2pm again, forever. What is the BEST fallback design?

options.text: Detect the repeated contradiction, cap the retries, and return the best consistent plan found so far
Let it keep flip-flopping until the user closes the app
Delete the whole schedule and give no answer at all
Make the search infinitely deeper so it never stops

exercises.explain: Good agents assume reasoning can fail. The best fallback spots the contradiction or loop, sets a limit on retries, and returns the best consistent result instead of crashing or spinning forever. That's what makes a planning agent reliable.
